title: The human hepatocyte TXG-MAPr: gene co-expression network modules to support mechanism-based risk assessment
claims: TXG-MAPr webtool uses WGCNA from PHH TG-GATEs dataset to reveal mechanistic interpretation through gene co-expression network modules
The tool captures stress response pathways perturbed by specific stressors and shows preservation in rat systems except for DNA damage and oxidative stress responses
Module responses can be calculated from external datasets, enabling biological response imputation from a limited gene set
TXG-MAPr identifies higher basal level of intrinsic immune response in donors with pre-existing liver pathology

Outer-medulla proximal tubules show sustained DNA damage response, inflammation, actin cytoskeletal remodeling, increased glycolytic metabolism, and suppression of mitochondrial activity.
Later responses include regeneration-related cell cycle pathway activation and ribosomal biogenesis in injured outer-medulla proximal tubules.
Whole kidney transcriptome analysis is less sensitive than OMPT for detecting nephrotoxicity mechanisms.
summary: The study uses LCM and TempO-Seq to reveal detailed spatio-temporal mechanisms of renal injury and regeneration in response to cisplatin, identifying outer-medulla proximal tubules as the most representative nephron segment for biomarker detection.

title: Potentially Toxic Elements (PTEs) in Plants and Animals: Environmental Pathways, Toxicity Mechanisms, and Biomonitoring Insights
claims: PTEs such as arsenic, cadmium, lead, chromium, mercury, zinc, and copper traverse abiotic compartments and biological kingdoms causing molecular dysfunction, ecosystem disruption, and human health impacts.
Essential metals like copper and zinc become toxic above permissible limits while nonessential metals like lead and cadmium exert toxicity even at trace levels.

Cross-kingdom commonalities in PTEs include oxidative stress, enzyme inhibition, genotoxicity, with differences in exposure pathways and trophic transfer, such as mercury biomagnifying up to 10-fold in aquatic food webs.
Mechanistic endpoints are linked to population-level and ecosystem-level impacts.
Phytoremediation can achieve up to 80% removal efficiency for zinc and cadmium.

title: Drug-induced acute kidney injury: diverse mechanisms of tubular injury.
claims: Drugs cause acute kidney injury (AKI) through diverse mechanisms including transport and handling by renal cells, crystal formation, and immune-mediated damage.
Aminoglycosides induce AKI via endocytosis and pinocytosis in the proximal tubule.
Tenofovir and cisplatin cause intracellular drug accumulation leading to injury due to their specific transport mechanisms.

title: Current Levels of Environmental Exposure to Cadmium in Industrialized Countries as a Risk Factor for Kidney Damage in the General Population: A Comprehensive Review of Available Data

LOAELs for blood and urine Cd concentrations are >0.18 μg/L and >0.27 μg/g creatinine, which fall within the range observed in inhabitants of industrialized countries.
